did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a Computer Aided Detection (CAD) system for melanoma diagnosis using a fuzzy-based support system. It employs image processing techniques (segmentation, feature extraction based on ABCD-rule) and fuzzy logic membership functions (Gaussian, Bell, and Lagrange Interpolation Curve Fitting) to classify dermoscopic images into Malignant Melanoma, Atypical Melanoma, and Common Nevus classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel two-stage Computer Aided Detection (CAD) framework using a fuzzy-based support system with a new membership function based on Lagrange Interpolation Curve Fitting. It describes original experimental work, including dataset usage (PH2), segmentation, feature extraction, and comparative analysis of sensitivity and specificity against Gaussian and Bell functions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Fuzzy Based Support System for Melanoma Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-stage Computer Aided Detection (CAD) framework for melanoma diagnosis using the PH2 dermoscopic image dataset. The system employs image segmentation and feature extraction based on the ABCD-rule to analyze lesions across three classes: Malignant Melanoma, Atypical Melanoma, and Common Nevus. Instead of binary outputs, the framework utilizes fuzzy membership functions, including a novel method based on Lagrange Interpolation Curve Fitting, to provide analogue values representing lesion severity. The authors evaluated the performance of this proposed method against Gaussian and Bell membership functions using sensitivity and specificity metrics. Results indicated that the Lagrange Interpolation method achieved higher sensitivity for Malignant and Atypical Melanoma and higher specificity for Common Nevus compared to the other functions. The study concludes that providing analogue fuzzy values aids dermatologists in decision-making by recreating clinical suspiciousness.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset used for diagnosis; 40 lesions in the dataset; classified as severe skin cancer.
One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset used for diagnosis; 80 lesions in the dataset; also known as Dysplastic Nevus.
One of the three classes in the PH2 dataset used for diagnosis; 80 lesions in the dataset; considered benign.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma classes (Malignant Melanoma, Atypical Melanoma, Common Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a Computer Aided Detection (CAD) system for classifying dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: Malignant Melanoma, Atypical Melanoma, and Common Nevus. Although the system outputs analogue fuzzy membership values representing the extent of a lesion belonging to each class to aid decision-making, the fundamental task is multi-class classification. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity and specificity metrics for these classes.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset acquired and managed by the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano (Matosinhos, Portugal). Images were acquired under similar conditions using a Tuebinger Mole Analyzer system with 20x magnification.

use.training: Used to create grouped frequency distributions for training features and to derive membership curves for the Lagrange Interpolation method and parameters for Gaussian/Bell functions.
use.testing: Used to evaluate fuzzy membership values and calculate sensitivity and specificity for the three membership functions (Gaussian, Bell, Lagrange).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity and specificity metrics without statistical significance testing.

PH2.metric: Sensitivity and Specificity for Malignant Melanoma (MM), Atypical Melanoma (AM), and Common Nevus (CN)
PH2.notes: The Lagrange Interpolation method achieved higher sensitivity for Malignant Melanoma and Atypical Melanoma compared to Gaussian. It also achieved higher specificity for Common Nevus compared to Gaussian. Lagrange had higher sensitivity than Bell for Atypical Melanoma and Common Nevus, and higher specificity than Bell for Malignant Melanoma and Atypical Melanoma. Gaussian and Bell functions produced very low membership values due to low correlation among features.

architecture_used.main_model: Fuzzy Based Support System (two-stage Computer Aided Detection framework)

stage_one.description: Image processing stage involving segmentation and feature extraction
stage_one.segmentation_algorithm: Algorithm by Abuzaghleh et al. [1] for PH2 dataset
preprocessing_steps: Hair removal using dull razor algorithm [7]
Final morphological open and close operations with mask M2

features: Asymmetry about major axis (ratio of non-overlapping area to total area)
feature_categories.calculation_details: Moment of inertia minimization, center of mass calculation, orientation angle theta

features: Ratio of total area matching blue, black, and white colors to grand total area
feature_categories.calculation_details: Euclidean distance measure for color matching

steps: Create grouped frequency distribution for each training feature vector (5 groups)
Obtain sample data (x: feature vector, y: normalized height)
Bound curves along x-axis (min/max of feature) and y-axis (0 to 1)
Calculate combined membership value as average of 9 feature membership values

training_details.dataset_split: PH2 dataset partitioned 75:25 into training and test sets
training_details.training_data_usage: Used to create grouped frequency distributions for training features and derive membership curves/parameters
training_details.implementation_environment: MATLAB on Intel Core i5-3230M Processor 2.60 GHz, 8 GB RAM, Windows 8.1 Pro (x64)

classification_or_output_head.output_description: Analogue values representing the relative extent of a lesion belonging to each of the three classes (Malignant Melanoma, Atypical Melanoma, Common Nevus)
classification_or_output_head.aggregation_method: Average of individual feature membership values for each class

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Lagrange Interpolation Curve Fitting membership function
preprocessing_techniques: Convert RGB to grayscale using weighted sum (0.2989*R + 0.5870*G + 0.1140*B)
Hair removal using dull razor algorithm
Masking with mask M1 to remove white corners
Binary active contour method for segmentation
Area opening algorithm to remove objects smaller than 50 pixels
Final morphological open and close operations with mask M2
conclusions: The proposed two-stage Computer Aided Detection (CAD) framework using a fuzzy-based support system with Lagrange Interpolation Curve Fitting provides analogue fuzzy membership values rather than binary results, which aids dermatologists in decision-making by recreating clinical suspiciousness.
The Lagrange Interpolation method achieved higher sensitivity for Malignant Melanoma (90%) and Atypical Melanoma (80%) compared to Gaussian membership functions (80% and 35%, respectively).
The Lagrange Interpolation method achieved higher specificity for Common Nevus (90%) compared to Gaussian membership functions (60%).

The Lagrange Interpolation method demonstrated higher sensitivity than Bell membership functions for Atypical Melanoma and Common Nevus, and higher specificity than Bell functions for Malignant Melanoma and Atypical Melanoma.

Gaussian and Bell membership functions produced very small membership values due to low correlation among features belonging to the ABCD-rule, making them less suitable for fuzzy-based decision support systems that require relative extent information.

The framework fails in cases where the fuzzy membership value is relatively very high for the class to which the lesion does not belong, indicating a limitation that needs to be addressed in future work.
Future improvements may involve combining more features from ABCD classes to enhance sensitivity and specificity.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PH2 dataset, which consists of only 200 dermoscopic images (40 Malignant Melanoma, 80 Atypical Melanoma, 80 Common Nevus), limiting the generalizability of the findings.
The dataset was partitioned randomly into training (75%) and testing (25%) sets without mention of external validation or cross-validation, raising concerns about data leakage and overfitting.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported differences in sensitivity and specificity between the membership functions.

The Gaussian and Bell membership functions exhibited very low membership values due to low correlation among ABCD-rule features, suggesting these standard functions are poorly suited for this specific feature set.

The proposed Lagrange Interpolation method achieved high sensitivity for Malignant Melanoma (90%) and Atypical Melanoma (80%), but the clinical utility of analogue fuzzy values over binary outputs remains anecdotal and not empirically validated by dermatologists.
The system has a noted limitation where it may produce relatively very high membership values for an incorrect class, indicating potential reliability issues in ambiguous cases.
Feature extraction is limited to 9 features based on the ABCD-rule, potentially ignoring other relevant dermoscopic criteria or contextual information.
